summary: This brief link roundup post touches on several topics: Microsoft's .NET marketing failures, small font sizes in web design, and CityDesk product news. Spolsky references CNet's criticism of Microsoft's .NET marketing campaign, noting he had written about the same issue two years earlier. He concedes that while .NET was primarily a marketing exercise rather than a technological revolution, the development tools did make impressive progress. The post also highlights Jakob Nielsen's complaint about tiny text on websites and promotes a new Flash demo for Fog Creek's CityDesk product.
key_points: Microsoft's .NET branding was a marketing campaign gone wrong, not a genuine technological revolution, though the underlying tools did improve significantly
Spolsky's earlier criticism of .NET was about marketing hype, not the technology itself, and was frequently misunderstood
Jakob Nielsen's complaint about tiny text stems from Internet Explorer's inability to resize fonts when designers specify exact pixel sizes in CSS
money_quotes: My beef was that .NET was just marketing gone crazy, it wasn't a criticism of any particular piece of technology that got the .NET moniker stuck on it.

I also claimed that .NET was not revolutionary, but rather a new name for things that were under development anyway, although in retrospect I'm pretty impressed by just what a big step forward the languages and development tools took.
What Jakob is really complaining about is that Windows versions of IE do not give the user the ability to change the font size when the designer has specified an exact pixel size using CSS.
key_insight: Microsoft's .NET was primarily a marketing exercise that rebranded existing development efforts, though the underlying technology turned out to be a genuine step forward.
